enrichment.short_description: Postal is an open-source, self-hosted mail delivery platform for sending and receiving transactional email, built as an alternative to SendGrid or Mailgun.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Postal is a free, open-source mail server for self-hosting SMTP relay and inbound email, released under the MIT license.

features.title: SMTP relay and delivery
features.description: Send transactional and bulk email through your own infrastructure instead of a third-party ESP.

features.title: Inbound email handling
features.description: Receive and route incoming mail for connected domains.

features.title: Webhooks
features.description: Trigger events for message delivery, bounces, and other mail events for integration with other systems.

features.title: Web-based management UI
features.description: Administer mail servers, domains, and message queues from a browser.

features.title: Docker-based installation
features.description: Deploy Postal via Docker containers rather than a manual server build.

features.title: MIT licensed
features.description: Fully open source and free for commercial and private use.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Self-hosted, giving full control over mail infrastructure and data
Active project with 16.7k GitHub stars and regular releases (latest v3.3.7)
Webhooks support for integrating mail events into other systems
cons: Requires self-managed server infrastructure, Docker, and Ruby to run
No official first-party hosted/managed version
Deliverability depends on the operator's own IP reputation and DNS setup

faqs.question: Is Postal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Postal is open source and released under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Does Postal offer a hosted version?
faqs.answer: The official project is self-hosted via Docker; no first-party managed hosting is documented on the official site.

faqs.question: What does Postal replace?
faqs.answer: It's designed as a self-hosted alternative to commercial services like SendGrid or Mailgun.

faqs.question: Does Postal support webhooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides webhooks for mail delivery events.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted transactional email
use_cases.description: Run your own SMTP relay for application-generated email instead of paying a third-party ESP.

use_cases.title: Inbound email processing
use_cases.description: Receive and route incoming mail programmatically via webhooks.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Postal?
paragraphs: Postal is an open-source mail delivery platform that lets organizations run their own mail server for sending and receiving email, rather than relying on a commercial email service provider.
It is released under the MIT license, deployed via Docker, and built primarily with Ruby. The project has 16.7k GitHub stars and continues to ship regular releases, including v3.3.7.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Postal is for
paragraphs: Postal suits developers and DevOps teams who want a self-hosted, MIT-licensed alternative to services like SendGrid or Mailgun and are comfortable managing their own mail infrastructure via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: Mailcow is an open-source, Dockerized mail server suite combining Postfix, Rspamd, SOGo, and Nginx behind a web-based admin UI.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mailcow is a free, GPLv3-licensed Dockerized mail server suite with Postfix, Rspamd, and SOGo, plus optional paid professional support.

features.title: Dockerized deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker and Docker Compose for reproducible installs.

features.title: Postfix mail transfer agent
features.description: Handles sending and receiving mail for connected domains.

features.title: Rspamd spam filtering
features.description: Built-in spam filtering engine included in the stack.

features.title: SOGo groupware
features.description: Provides webmail, calendar, and address book functionality.

features.title: Nginx web component
features.description: Serves the admin panel and webmail interfaces.

features.title: Web-based administration UI
features.description: Manage domains, mailboxes, and settings from a browser.

pros: Free and open source (GPLv3)
Combines multiple mail components (MTA, spam filter, groupware, web server) into one managed stack
Large, active community with 13.1k GitHub stars
Optional paid professional support available via servercow.de
cons: Requires Docker/Docker Compose and a Debian-based host
DNS and SSL/TLS configuration must be handled by the operator
No official free hosted/cloud version, only self-hosting or paid professional support

faqs.question: Is Mailcow free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is free and open source under the GPLv3 license.

faqs.question: What components does Mailcow bundle?
faqs.answer: Postfix, Rspamd, SOGo, and Nginx, orchestrated via Docker.

faqs.question: Is professional support available?
faqs.answer: Yes, through The Infrastructure Company GmbH / servercow.de.

faqs.question: What OS does Mailcow require?
faqs.answer: A Debian-based system running Docker and Docker Compose.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted business email
use_cases.description: Run a full mail server stack with webmail and groupware for a company domain.

use_cases.title: Managed alternative via paid support
use_cases.description: Get professional support for a Mailcow deployment without building an in-house team.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mailcow?
paragraphs: Mailcow is an open-source, Dockerized mail server suite that bundles Postfix, Rspamd, SOGo, and Nginx into a single managed stack with a web-based admin UI.
It is licensed under GPLv3, has 13.1k GitHub stars, and offers optional paid professional support through servercow.de for teams that don't want to self-support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mailcow is for
paragraphs: Mailcow is aimed at IT admins and self-hosting teams who want a complete, Docker-based mail and groupware stack on a Debian-based server, with the option to buy professional support instead of handling everything in-house.

enrichment.short_description: Mailu is a free, open-source mail server distributed as Docker images, covering IMAP, SMTP, webmail, and spam/anti-spoofing protection.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mailu is a free, MIT-licensed Docker-based mail server with IMAP, SMTP, webmail, DKIM/DMARC, and antivirus, deployable via Compose or Kubernetes.

features.title: IMAP, SMTP, and Submission
features.description: Standard mail protocols with auto-configuration for clients.

features.title: Full-text search and aliases
features.description: Supports full-text search plus custom email forwarding and aliases.

features.title: Enforced TLS, DANE, and MTA-STS
features.description: Security protocols with Let's Encrypt certificate support.

features.title: DKIM signing and DMARC/SPF validation
features.description: Built-in email authentication and anti-spoofing protection.

features.title: Antivirus scanning
features.description: Blocks malicious attachments and scans incoming mail.

features.title: Auto-learning anti-spam with greylisting
features.description: Reduces spam without manual rule maintenance.

features.title: Web interfaces
features.description: Separate interfaces for end users and administrators.

features.title: Domain delegation and quotas
features.description: Global admin roles, per-domain delegation, and mailbox quotas.

pros: Free, MIT-licensed, fully open-source components
Supports both Docker Compose and Kubernetes deployment
Built-in antivirus, DKIM/DMARC, and anti-spoofing security
7.4k GitHub stars with active development
cons: Requires operator-managed DNS, TLS certificates, and a database backend
No official managed/hosted offering
Self-hosting requires ongoing maintenance and security patching

faqs.question: Is Mailu free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free software licensed under MIT.

faqs.question: What deployment options does Mailu support?
faqs.answer: Docker Compose and Kubernetes.

faqs.question: Does Mailu include antivirus and spam filtering?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are built in.

faqs.question: What databases does Mailu support?
faqs.answer: MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL, per the documentation.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted mail infrastructure
use_cases.description: Deploy a full-featured mail server via Docker Compose or Kubernetes.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused email hosting
use_cases.description: Run your own domain email with built-in DKIM, DMARC, and antivirus protections.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mailu?
paragraphs: Mailu is a free, open-source mail server distributed as a set of Docker images, providing IMAP, SMTP, webmail, and security features like DKIM signing, DMARC/SPF validation, and antivirus scanning.
It is MIT licensed, has 7.4k GitHub stars, and can be deployed via Docker Compose or Kubernetes with MySQL, MariaDB, or PostgreSQL as the database backend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mailu is for
paragraphs: Mailu fits DevOps teams and self-hosters who want a container-native, fully open-source mail server with strong built-in security defaults and no vendor lock-in.

enrichment.short_description: Modoboa is an open-source email server platform offering webmail, unlimited domains and mailboxes, and admin tools, installable in minutes.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Modoboa is a free, ISC-licensed open-source email server with webmail, calendars, and unlimited domains, powering 800,000+ mailboxes.

features.title: Browser-based webmail
features.description: Access mail directly from a web browser.

features.title: Calendar and address book
features.description: Built-in calendar and contacts management.

features.title: Filtering rules and auto-responder
features.description: Configure email filtering rules and out-of-office replies.

features.title: Unlimited domains and mailboxes
features.description: No software-imposed cap on domains, mailboxes, or aliases created.

features.title: Admin statistics and migration tools
features.description: Built-in tools for administrators to monitor usage and migrate data.

features.title: Fast installer
features.description: The site states installation takes less than 10 minutes.

pros: Free and open source (ISC license)
Reports over 800,000 mailboxes deployed
Fast installation process (claimed under 10 minutes)
Unlimited domains, mailboxes, and aliases with no software-imposed cap
cons: Hands-on professional support requires paid services
Requires the operator to configure DNS after installation
Self-hosted only; no official managed cloud version

faqs.question: Is Modoboa free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is free and open source (ISC license).

faqs.question: How long does installation take?
faqs.answer: The site states installation takes less than 10 minutes.

faqs.question: Is paid support available?
faqs.answer: Yes, via Modoboa.com, with installation/upgrade services starting from €150 and issue resolution from €120.

faqs.question: How many mailboxes does Modoboa power?
faqs.answer: The site cites more than 800,000 mailboxes.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email for organizations
use_cases.description: Run unlimited domains and mailboxes on your own infrastructure with webmail included.

use_cases.title: Assisted deployment via paid services
use_cases.description: Use Modoboa's paid installation and maintenance services instead of self-managing setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Modoboa?
paragraphs: Modoboa is an open-source email server platform that lets anyone install their own email server with webmail, calendars, and unlimited domains and mailboxes.
It is ISC licensed, has 3.5k GitHub stars, and the project's site claims more than 800,000 mailboxes are running on Modoboa deployments worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Modoboa is for
paragraphs: Modoboa targets both technical self-hosters who want a fast, unlimited-mailbox installation and less technical users who can pay for Modoboa.com's professional installation, troubleshooting, and maintenance services.

enrichment.short_description: Stalwart is a Rust-native, open-source mail and collaboration server supporting JMAP, IMAP, and SMTP, with a paid Enterprise edition.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stalwart is an open-source (AGPL-3.0) Rust mail server with JMAP/IMAP/SMTP and CalDAV/CardDAV; Enterprise pricing starts at €2/mailbox/year.

features.title: JMAP, IMAP, POP3, and SMTP
features.description: Supports multiple mail access and transfer protocols.

features.title: CalDAV, CardDAV, and WebDAV
features.description: Built-in calendar, contacts, and file storage collaboration protocols.

features.title: Spam and phishing protection
features.description: Statistical filtering combined with AI-based analysis.

features.title: Encryption at rest
features.description: Supports S/MIME and OpenPGP for stored message encryption.

features.title: DANE, MTA-STS, and automatic TLS
features.description: Transport security protocols configured automatically.

features.title: Clustering
features.description: Scale the server across multiple nodes.

features.title: Pluggable storage backends
features.description: Supports RocksDB, PostgreSQL, S3, Azure Blob, and other backends.

pros: Community edition is open source (AGPL-3.0)
Combines email, calendar, contacts, and file storage in one server
Rust-native, with 13.7k GitHub stars
Enterprise edition includes unlimited domains, tenants, mailing lists, and aliases at no extra cost
cons: Enterprise edition uses a proprietary license (Stalwart Enterprise License v2), not open source
Enterprise pricing is per-mailbox and scales with deployment size
Premium support is only included starting at 150+ mailboxes on Enterprise

faqs.question: Is Stalwart free?
faqs.answer: The Community edition is free and open source under AGPL-3.0; the Enterprise edition is paid.

faqs.question: What does Enterprise pricing look like?
faqs.answer: It's tiered per mailbox, starting at €2.00/mailbox/year for 25-499 mailboxes and dropping in price at higher tiers, with custom quotes above 100,000 mailboxes.

faqs.question: What protocols does Stalwart support?
faqs.answer: JMAP, IMAP, POP3, SMTP, plus CalDAV/CardDAV/WebDAV.

faqs.question: Can Stalwart cluster across nodes?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports clustering for scaling across multiple nodes.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email and collaboration
use_cases.description: Run mail, calendar, and contacts on one Rust-based server using the open-source Community edition.

use_cases.title: Enterprise mail hosting at scale
use_cases.description: Deploy the paid Enterprise edition for per-mailbox licensing with premium support at larger mailbox counts.
use_cases.audience: Organizations needing supported, scalable mail infrastructure

features: Unlimited domains, tenants, mailing lists and aliases
Unlimited domains, tenants, mailing lists and aliases
Unlimited domains, tenants, mailing lists and aliases

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Stalwart Mail Server?
paragraphs: Stalwart is a Rust-native mail and collaboration platform that combines email (JMAP/IMAP/POP3/SMTP), calendar and contacts (CalDAV/CardDAV), and file storage (WebDAV) in a single server.

The Community edition is open source under AGPL-3.0 with 13.7k GitHub stars, while an Enterprise edition adds premium support and is licensed separately under the Stalwart Enterprise License v2, priced per mailbox per year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Stalwart Mail Server is for
paragraphs: Stalwart suits self-hosters who want a single Rust-based server for mail and collaboration, as well as organizations that want per-mailbox Enterprise pricing with premium support instead of maintaining a purely community-supported stack.

enrichment.short_description: Maddy is a free, open-source, composable all-in-one mail server written in Go that consolidates SMTP, IMAP, and mail security into one daemon.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Maddy Mail Server is a free, GPL-3.0-licensed Go mail server combining SMTP, IMAP, DKIM, SPF, DMARC, and DANE into a single daemon.

features.title: Combined SMTP/MTA and SMTP/MX
features.description: Handles both sending and receiving mail in one daemon.

features.title: Built-in IMAP storage
features.description: Provides IMAP access to stored mail, currently labeled beta by the developers.

features.title: DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, MTA-STS
features.description: Implements common email authentication and transport security protocols.

features.title: Docker support
features.description: Can be deployed using Docker.

features.title: Single-daemon architecture
features.description: Replaces separate tools like Postfix, Dovecot, and OpenDKIM with one process.

pros: Free and open source (GPL-3.0)
Consolidates multiple mail server roles into a single Go binary/daemon
6,000 GitHub stars, actively maintained
Built-in email authentication (DKIM/SPF/DMARC/DANE/MTA-STS)
cons: IMAP storage is labeled "beta" by the developers, who recommend Dovecot for production storage
No documented specific OS/system requirements on the official site content reviewed
Community-only support (IRC, mailing list), no paid support tier

faqs.question: Is Maddy free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Is Maddy production-ready for IMAP storage?
faqs.answer: The developers describe IMAP storage as "beta" and suggest pairing with Dovecot for production storage.

faqs.question: What does Maddy replace?
faqs.answer: It replaces separate tools like Postfix, Dovecot, and OpenDKIM with one daemon.

faqs.question: Does Maddy support Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, Docker deployment is supported.

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosted mail server
use_cases.description: Run SMTP sending/receiving and mail security (DKIM/SPF/DMARC) from a single Go daemon.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosters wanting a simpler alternative to multi-tool mail stacks

use_cases.title: Mail delivery focus
use_cases.description: Use Maddy primarily for message transmission while pairing with Dovecot for IMAP storage.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Maddy Mail Server?
paragraphs: Maddy is a composable, all-in-one mail server written in Go that consolidates message transmission, reception, and IMAP storage into a single daemon, along with DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS.
It is open source under GPL-3.0 with 6,000 GitHub stars. The developers note that its IMAP storage component is still labeled beta and recommend Dovecot for production storage needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Maddy Mail Server is for
paragraphs: Maddy fits self-hosters and developers who want a single Go binary handling mail delivery and security protocols instead of wiring together Postfix, Dovecot, and OpenDKIM separately.

enrichment.short_description: Mail-in-a-Box is a free, CC0-licensed script that turns a fresh Ubuntu cloud server into a full self-hosted mail server in a few hours.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mail-in-a-Box is a free, public-domain (CC0) tool that installs a complete self-hosted mail server with webmail, DNS, and TLS on Ubuntu 22.04.

features.title: Webmail via Roundcube
features.description: Browser-based email access included out of the box.

features.title: Contacts and calendar sync
features.description: Uses Nextcloud for contacts and calendar synchronization.

features.title: Web-based control panel
features.description: Manage user accounts through a control panel.

features.title: Automated DNS configuration
features.description: Sets up SPF, DKIM, DMARC, MTA-STS, and DNSSEC automatically.

features.title: Automatic TLS certificates
features.description: Provisions certificates via Let's Encrypt.

features.title: Spam filtering with greylisting
features.description: Reduces unwanted mail without manual configuration.

features.title: Static website hosting
features.description: Can also serve a static website from the same box.

features.title: Two-factor authentication and REST API
features.description: Adds account security and programmatic access.

features.title: Automated backups
features.description: Backs up to Amazon S3 and other supported storage services.

pros: Free and released under the CC0 public domain dedication, with no license restrictions
Single-command setup targeted at non-experts
15.4k GitHub stars, indicating broad adoption
Automates DNS, TLS, and backup configuration, reducing manual setup work
cons: Requires a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 LTS 64-bit cloud server, not existing/shared servers
Installation takes "a few hours" per the official site
Self-hosted only; no official hosted/managed version

faqs.question: Is Mail-in-a-Box free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's released under the CC0-1.0 public domain dedication.

faqs.question: What server does it require?
faqs.answer: A fresh Ubuntu 22.04 LTS 64-bit machine running in the cloud.

faqs.question: How is it installed?
faqs.answer: Via a single setup command: curl -s https://mailinabox.email/setup.sh | sudo bash.

faqs.question: Does it include webmail?
faqs.answer: Yes, via Roundcube, plus contacts/calendar sync via Nextcloud.

use_cases.title: Turnkey self-hosted email
use_cases.description: Turn a fresh cloud VM into a working mail server without deep sysadmin expertise.
use_cases.audience: Individuals and small organizations self-hosting email

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused personal email
use_cases.description: Run your own domain's email, contacts, and calendar independent of third-party providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mail-in-a-Box?
paragraphs: Mail-in-a-Box is a free, public-domain script that turns a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 LTS cloud server into a complete, working mail server, including webmail, contacts/calendar sync, DNS automation, and TLS certificates.
It is released under the CC0-1.0 dedication, has 15.4k GitHub stars, and installs via a single setup command that takes a few hours to complete.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mail-in-a-Box is for
paragraphs: Mail-in-a-Box targets individuals and small organizations who want to self-host email without deep systems administration expertise, thanks to its heavily automated DNS, TLS, and backup setup.

enrichment.short_description: Mailpit is a free, open-source, single-binary email testing tool with a web UI, SMTP server, REST API, and HTML/CSS compatibility checks.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mailpit is a free, MIT-licensed email testing tool for developers with a web UI, SMTP capture, REST API, and spam-score testing.

features.title: Single binary, zero dependencies
features.description: Deploy as one executable with no external dependencies.

features.title: Live web UI
features.description: Real-time notifications as new captured messages arrive.

features.title: HTML/CSS compatibility checker
features.description: Checks email markup against client compatibility rules.

features.title: Link validation and screenshots
features.description: Validates links and generates screenshots of rendered emails.

features.title: Spam testing
features.description: Integrates with SpamAssassin for spam-score testing.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access to captured messages for test automation.

features.title: POP3 server support
features.description: Exposes captured mail over POP3 in addition to the web UI.

features.title: Message tagging, relaying, and forwarding
features.description: Organize and route captured test messages.

pros: Free and open source (MIT license)
Single binary with zero dependencies, simple to deploy
9.9k GitHub stars
Includes a REST API and webhook integration for test automation
cons: Intended for email testing, not production mail delivery
No official pricing/support tiers; community-supported only
Frontend built with Go/Vue, requiring build tooling to customize

faqs.question: Is Mailpit free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What is Mailpit used for?
faqs.answer: It's an SMTP testing tool for developers to capture and inspect outgoing email during development.

faqs.question: Does Mailpit have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a REST API for programmatic access.

faqs.question: Can Mailpit test for spam scoring?
faqs.answer: Yes, via SpamAssassin integration.

use_cases.title: Local email testing
use_cases.description: Capture and preview outgoing application email during development without sending real messages.

use_cases.title: CI/CD email testing
use_cases.description: Use the REST API to assert on captured emails within automated test suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Mailpit?
paragraphs: Mailpit is a free, open-source email testing tool for developers: a small, zero-dependency single binary that runs an SMTP server plus a web UI and REST API for capturing and inspecting outgoing email.
It is MIT licensed, has 9.9k GitHub stars, and adds developer-focused extras like HTML/CSS client compatibility checks, link validation, screenshot generation, and SpamAssassin-based spam testing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Mailpit is for
paragraphs: Mailpit is built for developers and QA/DevOps engineers who need to capture and verify outgoing application email in local development or CI/CD pipelines without sending real messages.

enrichment.short_description: MJML is a free, open-source markup language created by Mailjet in 2015 that compiles into responsive, cross-client HTML email.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MJML is a free, MIT-licensed responsive email markup language from Mailjet that transpiles into cross-client HTML with inlined CSS.

features.title: Semantic markup tags
features.description: Uses tags like mj-section and mj-column instead of raw HTML tables.

features.title: Responsive by default
features.description: Output is responsive across major email clients, including Outlook.

features.title: Component-based architecture
features.description: Reusable, extensible native components.

features.title: Custom components
features.description: Build your own components beyond the native set.

features.title: HTML transpilation
features.description: Compiles MJML into responsive HTML with nested tables and inline CSS.

features.title: Built with React.js
features.description: The framework's tooling is built for high composability using React.js.

pros: Free and open source (MIT license)
18.2k GitHub stars, one of the most widely adopted email markup frameworks
Simplifies notoriously fragile HTML email coding
Extensible with custom components
cons: Requires a build/compile step to transpile MJML into HTML
No official hosted editor or paid tier found on the official site
Learning a markup language adds overhead versus writing raw HTML for very simple emails

faqs.question: Is MJML free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Who created MJML?
faqs.answer: Mailjet, released in 2015 during work on their Passport drag-and-drop email builder.

faqs.question: Does MJML guarantee cross-client compatibility?
faqs.answer: It's designed to be responsive by default across major email clients, including Outlook.

faqs.question: Can I extend MJML with custom components?
faqs.answer: Yes, its component-based architecture supports custom, reusable components.

use_cases.title: Responsive transactional/marketing email
use_cases.description: Author email templates in a simplified markup language that compiles to cross-client-safe HTML.

use_cases.title: Reusable email component libraries
use_cases.description: Build custom, reusable MJML components for consistent email design across a product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What is MJML?
paragraphs: MJML is a responsive email framework created by Mailjet in 2015 that uses a semantic markup language, transpiled into responsive HTML with nested tables and inline CSS, to simplify the notoriously fragile process of coding HTML email.
It is open source under the MIT license, has 18.2k GitHub stars, and is built with a component-based architecture that includes both native and custom-buildable components.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who MJML is for
paragraphs: MJML is for frontend developers and product teams who build responsive HTML email templates and want a semantic markup layer plus a build step instead of hand-coding nested HTML tables and inline CSS.

enrichment.short_description: Unlayer is a developer platform providing embeddable email, page, document, and popup builders with a free plan and paid usage-based tiers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Unlayer lets developers embed drag-and-drop email, page, and document builders into their apps, from a free plan up to a $2,000/month Optimize plan.

features.title: Visual builders
features.description: Drag-and-drop email, page, document, and popup builders.

features.title: AI workflows
features.description: Content generation, rewriting, personalization, and translation.

features.title: Branded templates
features.description: Create and manage branded templates for end users.

features.title: Multi-format export
features.description: Export designs to HTML, PDF, images, and plain text.

features.title: Developer SDK and APIs
features.description: SDK, REST APIs, React Elements, and a CLI for integrating builders into a product.

features.title: Media management
features.description: Asset upload and organization within the builders.

features.title: White-labeling
features.description: Available on paid plans for embedding under a customer's own brand.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Available on the Scale plan and above.

pros: Free plan available with core email/page/popup/document builders
SOC 2 Type II compliant with a 99.9% uptime SLA
Used by 1,000+ SaaS brands including Mastercard, Bank of America, and Twilio, per Unlayer's site
Y Combinator-backed
14-day free trial on paid plans
cons: Paid plans start at $250/month, a significant jump from the free tier
Higher-value features (custom CSS, custom themes, AMP, inbox previews) are reserved for the $2,000/month Optimize plan
Usage-based add-ons (AI, export, inbox preview, bandwidth credits) cost extra, from $50 to $2,000/month, on top of plan fees
Enterprise pricing is custom/quote-only

faqs.question: Does Unlayer have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free plan with email, page, popup, and document builders is available at $0.

faqs.question: What do paid plans cost?
faqs.answer: Launch is $250/month, Scale is $750/month, and Optimize is $2,000/month, each with a 14-day free trial; Enterprise is custom-priced.

faqs.question: Is Unlayer SOC 2 compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's SOC 2 Type II compliant with a 99.9% uptime SLA.

faqs.question: Does Unlayer offer an annual discount?
faqs.answer: Yes, annual billing saves 10% versus monthly.

faqs.question: Who is Unlayer for?
faqs.answer: Developers and technical teams embedding content-creation tools, such as email/page/document builders, into their own SaaS products.

use_cases.title: Embedded email builder for SaaS
use_cases.description: Add a drag-and-drop email design tool inside a marketing platform or CRM product.
use_cases.audience: SaaS product teams (email service providers, CRMs, marketing platforms)

use_cases.title: White-labeled content creation tools
use_cases.description: Offer branded page, document, and popup builders to end customers via SDK/API integration.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted content generation
use_cases.description: Use built-in AI workflows to generate, rewrite, personalize, or translate email/page content.

features: Email, page, popup, and document builders

long_form_blocks.heading: What is Unlayer?
paragraphs: Unlayer is a developer platform that lets companies embed drag-and-drop email, page, document, and popup builders directly inside their own applications, so end users can create content without leaving the host product.

It offers a free plan plus paid Launch ($250/month), Scale ($750/month), and Optimize ($2,000/month) tiers, each with a 14-day free trial, and custom Enterprise pricing. The company states it is SOC 2 Type II compliant, offers a 99.9% uptime SLA, is backed by Y Combinator, and serves 1,000+ SaaS brands.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Unlayer is for
paragraphs: Unlayer is built for developers and technical teams at SaaS companies, such as marketing platforms, CRMs, and email service providers, who want to integrate content-creation experiences into their product via SDK, REST API, or React Elements rather than building a builder from scratch.
